our_approach: Population-level trait variation across Yangtze water bodies + taxonomic validation via trait congruence

key_references.citation: Luiz OJ, Olden JD, Kennard MJ, et al. (2022) Substantial intraspecific trait variation across a hydrological gradient in northern Australian fishes. Ecosphere 13:e4169
key_references.finding: Intraspecific variability contributes up to 70% of total trait variability, averaging 31% across all traits. Most trait-based studies IGNORE this.
key_references.relevance: Validates our approach of recording traits per water body. We are applying this framework to Yangtze — first such application.

key_references.citation: Guo H, Zhang X, Tang W, Liu K (2025) Spatial and Sex-Specific Growth Variations of Migratory Coilia nasus in the Middle and Lower Yangtze, China. Biology 14(9):1211
key_references.finding: Coilia nasus shows significant growth variation across Yangtze sections. Upstream individuals are smaller but more fecund. VBGF parameters vary spatially.
key_references.relevance: Liu Kai group publication — directly supports our population-level approach. We have this data in our database.

key_references.citation: 长江中上游3种鲌的营养生态位比较 (2020) 中国水产科学 30(2):236
key_references.finding: δ13C and δ15N values of 3 Culter species differ significantly across upper/middle Yangtze reaches. Trophic niche varies by location.
key_references.relevance: Supports our multi-water-body trait recording for Culter species (翘嘴鲌/蒙古鲌/尖头鲌).

key_references.citation: Langerhans RB, Layman CA, Langerhans AK, Dewitt TJ (2003) Habitat-associated morphological divergence in two Neotropical fish. Biological Journal of the Linnean Society 80:689-698
key_references.finding: River channel vs lagoon habitats drive significant morphological divergence within the same species.
key_references.relevance: Classic reference for habitat-driven intraspecific morphological variation. Our Yangtze section comparison is analogous.

novelty_assessment.what_exists: Intraspecific trait variation studied in Australian, Neotropical, and European freshwater fish. Mostly ecological gradient studies.
novelty_assessment.what_is_new: 1) First application to Yangtze fish community at multi-species scale; 2) Combining population-level traits with taxonomic hypothesis testing (old vs new classification); 3) Integration with fishing ban impact assessment; 4) Chinese + IUCN conservation dual-framework.
novelty_assessment.potential_contribution: If published: 'Intraspecific trait variation as a tool for taxonomic validation: A case study from the Yangtze River fish community'. Target journals: Ecology and Evolution, Freshwater Biology, or Reviews in Fish Biology and Fisheries.
